key_insight: A temporal machine learning time-to-event framework can combine longitudinal ALS function scores with survival modeling to predict individualized disease progression and wheelchair-free survival.
clinical_relevance: This could help clinicians and care teams anticipate functional decline earlier, support proactive planning, and tailor follow-up for people with ALS.
method: Built a harmonized longitudinal ALS dataset from diagnosis, ALSFRS-R, daily living, and demographic records.
Clustered ALSFRS-R items into functional domains and modeled nonlinear decline with generalized additive mixed models.
Used temporal machine learning plus Cox proportional hazards modeling to generate individualized survival curves and predict assistive device use.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a cohort-based modeling pipeline with temporal alignment and survival analysis, identifying lower-limb function as the strongest predictor of earlier wheelchair access.
watch_item: Performance and generalizability may be limited if the model is applied to different clinics, data capture patterns, or ALS populations without external validation.
paper_title: A Temporal Machine Learning-Based Time-to-Event Model for Predicting ALS Progression and Healthcare Utilization
